title: Multitask Prompted Training Enables Zero-Shot Task Generalization

structured.summary: This paper explores whether explicit multitask prompted training enhances zero-shot generalization in large language models compared to implicit multitask learning. The authors fine-tune a T5 encoder-decoder model on a varied multitask mixture and demonstrate notable performance on unseen tasks, often exceeding that of larger models.

strengths.evidence: The model significantly outperformed GPT-3 on 7 out of 10 held-out datasets and surpassed a larger baseline model on 13 out of 14 comparable tasks in the BIG-Bench benchmark.
Authors converted a broad array of NLP tasks into prompted formats using a structured templating language, facilitating effective multitask training.

weaknesses.evidence: The process for selecting, converting datasets into prompts, and ensuring no knowledge overlap with held-out tasks is not well-explained.
The paper mainly relies on benchmark comparisons without exploring which prompts are more effective or why, lacking ablation studies to understand component contributions.
The explanation of what constitutes a 'prompt' and examples are not provided in-depth, potentially leading to confusion on replicability.

questions.question: How does the system ensure no training data overlaps with held-out tasks?
questions.motivation: Understanding the mechanism to maintain the integrity of zero-shot evaluations is crucial for validating claims of generalization.

critique_points.point_001: Lack of detailed methodology. The process for selecting, converting datasets into prompts, and ensuring no knowledge overlap with held-out tasks is not well-explained
critique_points.point_002: Limited evaluation metrics. The paper mainly relies on benchmark comparisons without exploring which prompts are more effective or why, lacking ablation studies to understand component contributions
critique_points.point_003: Insufficient clarity on prompts. The explanation of what constitutes a 'prompt' and examples are not provided in-depth, potentially leading to confusion on replicability
